q: In a Spark stream-stream join, what state does Spark maintain on each side and why does it grow unbounded if you omit watermarks?
a: When two streaming DataFrames are joined, Spark cannot know at processing time whether a row from stream A will eventually match a row from stream B that hasn't arrived yet. So it must buffer all past rows from BOTH streams in state storage (the RocksDB or HDFS state store) and attempt to match each new incoming row against every buffered row on the other side. Without watermarks and event-time range constraints there is no signal telling Spark that a buffered row can never match a future row — so every row is kept forever. State grows as O(N * M) where N and M are the row counts of each stream since query start, eventually exhausting memory or disk. To bound state you must: (1) declare withWatermark on both streams' event-time columns; (2) add a time-range join condition (e.g. clickTime BETWEEN impressionTime AND impressionTime + INTERVAL 1 HOUR). Together these let Spark calculate a cleanup bound: once the global watermark has advanced past the latest possible match time for a buffered row, that row is evicted from state. Without BOTH of these, state is not cleaned up even for inner joins.
fj: I'd add a watermark on one stream; that should be enough to stop state from growing.
fs: One watermark on one stream is not enough. Spark requires watermarks on BOTH input streams plus an event-time range join condition to derive a cleanup bound. A single watermark gives Spark no way to know when rows on the other (un-watermarked) side are safe to drop.

What metrics in the Spark UI show you that state is actually being evicted?
Does switching the state store backend from HDFS to RocksDB change the growth behavior, or just performance?
